desc:
Straddling a wide upturn of terrain between two deep fissures, the downs find
themselves in a strange position. North and south the land is steadily eroding
away, as the rainwater gathering in pools at the bottom eats away at the rocky
cliffsides. Still, there is a large swathe of downs left, half a mile wide, and
seems to be thriving. The ground it is based on must be quite fertile, as it is
covered in lush grass and countless varieties of small bush.
name: Downs among the Plains

desc:
The land rises up here slightly, then collapses into a yawning fissure to the
south. Far too steep to descend down into, it is rather out of place in these
normally flat plains. Across the fissure the grasslands continue as before,
rolling onwards to the base of the White Mountains. Some distance to the east of
here a path can be glimpsed, going north to south. It isn't the largest trail to
be found though, and you cannot see where it might be going.
name: Broken Windswept Plains

desc:
Long ago, when it was the link between Arnor and Gondor, the South Road was
frequented by countless merchants, soldiers and many other travellers. The
watering holes every few dozen miles were essential for the long trek through
Enedwaith, where dozens of miles can go between natural sources of water. This
one has lost most of the brickwork, but it is still easy to see the form. It is
a circular well, ten feet across, made from huge cubes of stone. Somehow the
engineers of old managed to determine where the water was most likely to flow
underground, an art long lost.
name: Water-hole by the Road

desc:
Cracked stones pave the road, crumbled away in places to expose the smaller
foundation stones below. Still, the skill of the makers can be seen, as the
raised camber of the road allows rain to run off in parts. A small path leads
away south from the road, leading to a ruined well in the plains, built long ago
to quench the thirst of travellers. Endless plains stretch away from the road,
away northeast to the snow-capped peaks of the Misty Mountains, and south to the
foothills of the White Mountains.
name: Long-Broken Remnants of a Road

desc:
The remains of the Great South Road lie here, little more than a line of strewn
stones running through the plains. Occasionally a larger uncracked flagstone can
be seen amongst the rubble, smooth as the day it was laid. Mostly however the
remains are a hindrance rather than an aid to travel, and quicker passage
through the lands could be made by travelling through the plains either side of
the road. Not far northwest of here the road crosses the wetlands on a raised
causeway, or what remains of it.
name: Crumbling Remains of the South Road

desc:
Not quite a hill, this small rise offers a good view of the land nearby. It is
covered in wild wheat and hay, about waist-height, with the sounds of various
animals rustling about in the growth. To the north there is a great deal of
farmland in the fertile plains, while south-west a town is visible atop a low,
flat hill. Along the south and west of this rise run the remains of an old road.
name: An Elevated Point in the Plains

desc:
Not long ago these fields must have been a good source of wheat but now they
have been abandoned and grow grass and hay, with a few small bushes giving some
variety to the terrain. The ground slopes upwards south to a small rise in the
plains, the ancient road skirting by to the south-west.
name: Abandoned Fields

desc:
There are several fields here, all different grazing areas. This is to ensure
the various farm animals don't wander into a field which is off rotation. Along
the fences there are troughs, where the animals get fresh water. In some of the
fields there are patches of wildflowers, but mostly they have been trampled. To
the west beyond the gate is a paddock, on the south side of the farm.
name: Grazing Land

desc:
Broken glass and crude wooden furniture are scattered about the floor here. The
Dunlendings that hunt in these lands sometimes use this old building as shelter
from the elements. Several centuries ago this was the inn where travellers on
the Great South Road stayed overnight, but it has been abandoned a long while
now, like the road. The bar is still standing at the far end of the room, where
a clerk probably sold the guest rooms and general supplies. There is a set of
stone stairs on the western wall, leading to the roofless upper level.
name: The Remains of an Inn

desc:
These rooms of the upper level are open to the elements now, the roof having
fallen away long ago. It is hard to imagine this was once an inn where guests
would relax during their journey on the Great South Road. The window frames are
empty, giving views over the wetlands to the west and the road north and east.
The stone floor is covered in all sorts of muck, from bird droppings to rubbish
thrown away by travellers.
name: A Guest Room

desc:
A rugged trail heads north to south here, through successive regions of scrub
and tall grasses. The ground is softer on the foot and the vegetation springs
back as one passes, leaving little sign by which to see tracks, but there are
markings of small animals leading to and from the bushes.
name: Among Tall Grass

desc:
A poorly marked mud trail heads off through the waving grasses towards the
south, weaving back and forth across the seemingly endless greensward. The
grasses range from two to three feet in height and seem to be even taller in the
distance.
name: A Rugged Trail

desc:
The road sweeps in a great curve towards the north and the east. It is in poor
shape, mostly broken and crumbled, except for the occasional flagstone which
remains whole and undamaged. They serve as the only lasting reminder of the
glorious past of the road's skilled builders.
name: Bend in the Great South Road

desc:
Overgrown and damaged through centuries of neglect, the Great South Road winds
its way through the open plains. The low, scrub bushes and brambles reach up
between cracked flagstones. Splintered and shattered by twisting roots, the
surface of the road is uneven and difficult to traverse.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The land is parched, and the plant life has adjusted to the harshness of the
exposed plain. The ground is covered by short, spiny-leaved grasses of a
yellowish-brown colour. Wherever there is a a hint of moisture upon the
splintered sandstone rock the grasses are tufted together, poking tenuously
forth from the sandy soil. The rocks are a bit larger towards the road further
south.
name: Arid Plains

desc:
A recently used trail passes through this area full of mixed scrubs and
grassland. There are stunted trees that show the marks of stripping by metal
tools. Also, there are many saplings that are scattered about the grassland. The
occasional bushel of eorna appears as though it was nurtured by a helping hand.
The plant itself resembles a form of barley and seems to be vibrant and in good
health, possibly cultivated.
name: Mixed Scrub

desc:
A trodden path heads north and south through a seemingly poor, but well-used
pasture. The taller grasses to the north appear cropped and cut short by local
herd animals. Their presence is evidenced by the telltale signs of noticeable
quantities of fresh manure scattered about the ground.
name: Rough Pasture

desc:
An obviously well tended field of eorna barley encompasses the landscape here.
It appears to be crudely irrigated and free of invasive scrubs. Southeast from
here lies a raised and sturdy wooden palisade wall that looms imposingly; ready
to keep out all but the most determined intruder.
name: A Field of Eorna

desc:
A Dunlending hold, constructed of stone and packed earth, looms menacingly to
the east. It is complete with a large wooden palisade. A great, bronze gate is
there to deny entrance to intruders. The dwellings within, as far as one can
see, are squat, windowless structures with wood framed roofs, covered in
stretched hides.
name: Before the Palisade

desc:
This field of cultivated potatoes, onions and turnips looks well cared for. The
irrigation channels have been cleverly dug to make the best use of the rain, as
well as making the hard job of keeping the crops healthy a little easier. The
wooden walls of a palisade loom to the northeast and a beaten path bends away
from the hold northward.
name: Crude Irrigation

desc:
A sturdy, sharp-pointed, timber wall rises up to the north, atop an earthen,
ramped base. The slope that is at the foot of the wall here, appears to have
been at some time, used as the tipping point for refuse from the other side. The
odour that is wafting from that direction seems to have attracted clouds of
flies. To the west, a mud trail passes through some irrigated fields, on its way
around the wood walled settlement to the north.
name: Below a Timber Wall

desc:
This shallow, watering hole seems to have been used quite a lot recently. The
water bed has been disturbed, kicking up mud and turning the water a mucky brown
colour. Animal tracks are in evidence all around it. A worn trail heads north
towards a wooden walled settlement.
name: A Muddy Watering Hole

desc:
The fortified settlement to the west seems to be surrounded by a wooden,
palisade wall with sharpened tips. A heavily built, postern gate provides a
possible entrance, but it seems as though it was built to keep out any visitors
that were not welcomed. A trail leads from the gate to a muddy, watering hole
that is nearby to the south.
name: Rear of a Palisade

desc:
A trail traverses through long-stemmed grasses here, which show faint signs of
having been cut back, not too long ago. To the southwest, a very high-walled,
wooden palisade stands atop an earth embankment. The palisade appears to be
surrounding a small, but seemingly active settlement.
name: A Hunting Trail

desc:
A large, wooden palisade wall, towering to the south, stands atop an embankment
of packed mud. The tips of the wall's timbers, have been sharpened to a point
and charred to withstand the elements. The timbers themselves seem to have been
coated with a muddy clay, to lessen the chance of them being set afire.
name: Outside a Crude Fort

desc:
The grass here has been nibbled on, almost to death. Nowhere is it any longer
than a finger or two in height, and in many places, the ground is bare earth.
Wild, grazing animals have ravaged the grasses and uprooted the few remaining
fungi and the roots of the fresh vegetation.
name: An Overgrazed Pasture

desc:
Tall grass grows here, ranging three or four feet in some places. The strong,
springy shoots have stretched upwards trying to outreach its neighbour in search
of the sun. At this areas eastern edge, however, there are signs of some recent
grazing and the grass is considerably shorter.
name: Tall Grasses

desc:
The tall grasses here have been blown into an eerie pattern of perfect circles,
most likely some peculiar trick of nature and the elements. The circles vary in
size from several feet, to several hundred feet and seem to all have been here
for some time. What their purpose is, or who may have laid them, seems to be a
mystery.
name: Windblown Circle

desc:
This verdant sward of grassland, just south of the Great South Road, has
considerably more vegetation than the poor, stony soil to the north. The
multitude of grasses are heavily interspersed with brambles and some low bushes,
providing a perfect habitat for insects and the smaller rodents.
name: Dense Grassland

desc:
This road was once a major thoroughfare, connecting the realms of Gondor and
Arnor. Now the passage of traffic has been reduced and the road has fallen into
a state of disrepair. In recent years, it has served as an age-old reminder of
the path that ancient travellers took. The easiest path along the road now is
the grass by its side rather than the road itself. Along the southern edge of
the road, an old drainage system is dug, preventing easy access to the lush
grasslands.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The vast, monotonous stretch of prairie is interrupted to the south by what
remains of the Great South Road. The grass is generally of a medium length,
except for where it seems to have been eaten short by animals. A soft rustling
of the grass in the wind invites the weary traveller to rest here.
name: Plains by the Road

desc:
A tufted carpet of short grasses envelopes this region, interspersed with the
occasional low, scrub bush and invasion of heather. This blanket rolls along at
a gradual slope for miles around. The ground underfoot here is very hard and
compact, carrying even faint noises a fair distance.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A field of hardy grain grows abundantly, unchecked on this wide level plain.
Resembling barley, the eorna plant has spread over a wide area and seems to be
uncontrolled by irrigation or fences. The fine grains of the barley emanate a
strong scent, undoubtedly attracting any nearby horses.
name: Wild Eorna Field

desc:
The stripped, barren land prevalent in this area gives way further to the north
to a final, craggy outcrop of the Misty Mountains far off in the distance. An
intrusion of granite stands prominently forth amidst the more numerous masses of
limestone boulders. There are signs of some ancient quarrying that are still
visible.
name: Rocky Footing

desc:
The ground is littered with rocks and pebbles of various sizes, making travel on
foot treacherous. The risk of a twisted or broken ankle seems high and the going
is slow. All around, the scenery here is barren and desolate, seemingly devoid
of all life.
name: Stony Ground

desc:
The land is barren and practically devoid of any form of plant life. With little
to hold the soil together, much of the upper layers have been blown away,
leaving stony, hard ground behind. Cracked, aged bones peek out from beneath the
remaining soil, bearing the only evidence the life that has inhabited this land.
name: Barren Flatland

desc:
The grass grows short and comparatively sparse here in contrast with the moist,
fertile soil further to the east. The soil is dry and infertile, making it to be
possible for only the heartiest grasses to survive. Moisture from the River Isen
does not reach the soil here. Further to the west, the land is even more barren
and unproductive.
name: Sparse Grassland

desc:
An ample supply of short, tufted grasses are sprinkled with rabbit pellets and
shallow burrows, along with numerous holes in the soft soil. There are soft,
light tufts of thrift and small shrubs, such as hounds-tongue, that have poked
up here and there, where the rabbits have left them to grow.
name: A Rabbit Patch

desc:
A carelessly placed foot can damage a blade of grass permanently, often killing
it. Haphazardly running over a field of grass will crush many blades of the
green vegetation. A whole herd of rampant animals, romping across a flat plain,
will usually crush and destroy all the tender blades of grass. That is what has
happened here it would seem.
name: Trampled Grass

desc:
This vast flatland is covered in thick grass, standing about five feet high,
that stretches for miles in all directions. The soil here is very rich and
fertile, with the potential for new life. However, the fierce winds that sweep
across this meadow, make it impossible for most of the plant life to flourish.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The vast, verdant green plains stretch out for many miles in every direction.
Occasionally, the living jade carpet underfoot is marred by the deep tracks of
many hoofed animals. The tracks lead away towards the southeast. Beyond the
beaten earth, the bright, snow-capped peaks of Ered Nimrais rise above the
horizon.
name: Western Plains

desc:
These vast plains spread far to the south and east, where plenty of the wild
creatures can feed off many forms of vegetation, which grow in this fertile
land. The grass is shorter here than in other areas, only reaching a foot in
height. The plains continue all around, ending only at the mountains that rise
up on the northern and southern horizons.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The Gap of Rohan stretches out in every direction, framed in the north and the
south by massive mountain ranges. Thick, vibrant jade grasses cover the plains
with barely a tree or bush in sight. If the slightest breeze blows, the blades
of grass bend in waves. Far in the east, an errant flicker of light can often be
seen in the mornings, possibly a reflection of water.
name: Western Plains

desc:
A dense carpet of grey-green grasses covers the gently undulating landscape. Few
distractions obstruct the view of the open land save a clearing in the east. A
medium-sized road in that direction leads north and appears to have seen recent
use. The road, however, lies several miles away.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
A well-kept dirt road breaks softly rolling plains to the east. The road bends
around this patch of green grass as it runs from the south, turns east, and then
continues north. On the other side of the road, the river Isen flows down from
the north and runs towards the distant peaks of Erid Nimrais.
name: Western Plains

desc:
This is a remarkably well kept dirt trail. No grass, weed or stone litter the
earth within the boundaries of this path. The earth is so tightly packed by the
repeated footfalls of man and beast, that even the harshest storms do not appear
to have an adverse effect. As the trail leads farther north, the dark peaks of
the Misty Mountains rise above the horizon. In the south, the path follows the
Isen towards the ford.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The river slowly beings to gain speed, as it flows directly south for many
miles. The water is clear and constantly cold no matter the season. The riverbed
lies many feet beneath the surface, but is perfectly visible. Small bleached
rocks line the riverbed, and are occasionally hidden by the large fish that swim
by. Atop the steep western bank, a dirt road follows the river's course.
name: River Isen

desc:
The waters flow fast and cold, even as the Isen bends to the west. As the river
flows farther south, the water cuts steeper and steeper banks along the river's
edge. The rocky bed lies many feet beneath the surface of the water, but can be
seen as easily as if it were mere inches away. There are places in the riverbed
where large ruts have formed, particularly near the edges. It appears that
someone has dug up large areas of the rock and sand mix.
name: River Isen

desc:
Gently undulating plains are abruptly halted by the swift waters of the Isen,
flowing just beyond its eastern bank here. For eons, the water has cut tall,
steep banks into the earth. Thick grass covers the slope, and remains wet both
day and night. Moving down those slopes is nearly impossible. Along the top of
the bank, repeated footfalls of both man and beast have worn down the grass to
almost nothing. This worn-down area follows the top of the riverbank very
closely.
name: East Bank

desc:
Vast, treeless plains gently roll to the east for more miles than mortal eyes
can see. Green waves slowly flow over the wide meads in even the gentlest of
winds. Tall blades of grass grow high enough to tickle the underbellies of the
mighty horses that wander these plains. In the warmer months, tiny purple
flowers can often be seen flourishing between the stalks of grass. In the
winter, all that remains are the grey-green stalks between the still thick
carpets of grass.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The plains are covered with a verdant grass, growing close to the ground. Many,
large stones and treacherous holes mark the ground, making travel through these
parts difficult. To the west and south, the grass grows taller in richer soil,
while springy and green it grows abundantly to the north.
name: Grassy Plains of Rohan

desc:
Some waist-high grass has covered the plains all around, a break from the broken
plains nearby. The nourishment of the river Isen is the reason, which is winding
its way through the flatlands just west of here, in a stony bed of rocks below.
It is marking the western borders of the Rohirrim, although, few currently live
in this part of their land.
name: Western Plains of Rohan

desc:
The waters of the Isen swirl around here, narrowing down from the north through
several small outcrops of rock. The current of the river does not change much
though, still a sedate meandering through the flat lands of the Gap. Its banks
are noticeably different in terrain, with the west side being comprised mostly
of rolling folds of land, while the east is mainly broken, with stony plains
which rise up out of this stony bed of rocks.
name: River Isen

desc:
The two most significant features of the landscape here are the river Isen,
flowing south in this section of Rohan, and the road that follows its path to
the west. Underfoot, the going is easy, with the soft turf making for an easy
ride or walk. These plains are mostly uninhabited, due to the frequent raids of
Dunlendings and orcs.
name: Plains between Isen and the Road

desc:
The ancient highway continues on its path here, following roughly the line of
the Isen a few miles east. Northward, it makes a route towards a deeply cut
valley between the two ends of the Misty Mountains, and south it appears to
travel across the central plains of the Gap of Rohan. Underfoot, there is no
paving, but the road is wide and well-kept, with the hard-packed earth making
for easy travel.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The ridge runs for miles north of here, slowly dissipating into a little more
than a grassy slope. The road runs at its base to the east, a wide and well
maintained trail. The land underfoot is as soft and easy on the traveller as
most of the grasslands here, terrain much suited to the liking of horses.
name: Last Remnants of the Northern Ridge

desc:
The wide, open land continues in every direction, with the flatlands comprising
the Gap of Rohan it would appear. Bound to the north and south by some of the
vast mountain ranges, it acts as a large, natural wind tunnel. Nevertheless, it
is a fertile country with short, verdant grass that serves as a springy carpet
for miles around.
name: Plains of the Gap

desc:
The land dips here, into a bowl-shaped depression. The vegetation has flourished
in this sheltered area, becoming thicker to the north where the depression
increases and blocks further passage. Little can be seen of the plains that
surround it, but the towering Misty Mountains to the north still raise their
peaks above the horizon.
name: A Slight Depression

desc:
A rotting wagon lies on its side in the midst of these rolling plains. The rear
axle of it is obviously broken and its wheels are missing. All of the metal
fittings appear to have been stripped or rusted, leaving no signs of who or what
may have left it here.
name: An Abandoned Wagon

desc:
The plains are broken by several low hillocks, covered in coarse grass and easy
to climb. Dandelions and buttercups cover much of the slopes, wherever the
grasses are short, breaking up the solid green with patches of yellow. Towards
the north, the grasses give way to coarser shrubs and nettles.
name: Low Rolling Hillocks

desc:
As the hillocks to the west come to an end, this area of raised ground provides
a perfect place to look out over the surrounding land. Southward, the River Isen
can be seen three to four miles distant. Beyond the broad river, some of the
foothills of the White Mountains begin their building ascent towards the
triple-crowned Thrihyrne. To the north and east there are grassy plains that
seem to stretch as far as the eye can see.
name: On Raised Ground

desc:
Rough meadow grasses, interspersed with dandelions, cover the majority of this
area of plains, dotting the green bedding with yellow. To the south, the River
Isen appears to begin to bend away to the southeast. Following its banks, one
can see the diverse vegetation that lines its passage.
name: A Field of Dandelions

desc:
A green cloak of rich grasses lies open, spangled with varying colours of wild
meadow flowers. In the distant past, this area must have been a managed flood
plain of grassland, harvested for hay by whomever lived here. To the south, the
cold waters of the Isen flow swiftly by.
name: An Ancient Meadow

desc:
Quiet plains, untouched by human interference, lie covered here in the wild
grasses and meadow of flowers. To the south, the River Isen can be seen winding
its way roughly westward. The plants lining the riverbanks are all quite tall
and more vibrant than elsewhere, a reflection of the abundant water.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A huge, carved limestone monolith thrusts upwards from a tufted grass hillock
amidst these desolate, ruined plains. The carvings upon the tall slab of this
stone appear intricate and expertly carved, though it would appear to have no
discernible significance. To the east and the south, the banks of the River Isen
bar any easy travel.
name: A Strange Monolith

desc:
The river runs clear, clean and swift. It seems to pick up speed as it turns
around a bend. This is an unusual occurrence for any river, as most tend to slow
where they bend. The only explanation is a change in water depth - the water is
fairly shallow here. As the river continues southward, the steep, sandy banks
turn into rocky cliffs.
name: River Isen

desc:
Clear, ice blue waters quickly flow around this bend and continue westward. The
riverbed, consisting mainly of a few small rocks and sand, is clearly visible,
as the waters are clean, yet deep. Fish dart by under the surface, most
following the current. The steep banks are alive with thick, green grass that
makes the slopes difficult to navigate.
name: River Isen

desc:
A well-travelled dirt trail follows the Isen's path as it flows southwest
through Rohan. The road is riddled with deep depressions. The trail is free of
weed or grass, a clear sign of its repeated use. The trail runs dangerously
close to the sharply sloping riverbank but appears stable enough to support
heavy weights.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
A well-travelled dirt trail follows the Isen's path through Rohan. The road is
covered with small holes and slight depressions - tell-tale signs of nature's
battle with man-made creations. Although the trail isn't very well tended, it is
still free of weed or grass, a clear sign of its repeated use. In the north, the
path runs towards the ford, while in the south, it heads towards to snow-capped
peaks of the White Mountains.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
Deep horse tracks are imprinted upon the path, an effect of long-term, repeated
use. There is not a sprig of greenery on the trail. No plant life could survive
the constant and harsh treatment this road receives. As the trail follows the
river's course, it sometimes runs very close the steep eastern bank. One misstep
could have very wet results.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
Deep, lush green grasses quickly spring up between two very different roads in
the east and west. To the east, an ancient stone roadway winds its way deeper
into the heart of Rohan. In the west, a worn dirt trail follows the Isen in its
trek towards the sea. Between these roads, a characteristic feature of Rohan
takes hold of the land once more: thick grey-green grasses.
name: Rough Grounds

desc:
As two roads diverge in the south, a wide wedge of green opens between them.
Knee-deep grasses cover the gently rolling plains as they stretch out towards
the wooded foothills of the White Mountains in the south. When the light is
waning at sunset, reflections off the distant waters of Sir Angren dance on the
western horizon. When the winds gently blow, the blades of grass move like waves
on a grey-green sea.
name: Rolling Mead

desc:
An ancient and weathered cobblestone roadway runs a direct path from the
strongholds of Rohan, to the beginning of Rohan territory at the Fords of Isen.
The widest stones of the pavement have been broken by the force of nature, and
repaired by the hands of man. Large, jagged rocks have been placed crudely, but
effectively, to repair the roadway and keep it functional. Those who restored
this road were not concerned about appearances however, as the repaired areas
are easily spotted, hasty patchwork as they are.
name: The West Road

desc:
The earth underfoot begins to rise slowly as one moves farther south. The
foothills of the White Mountains soon take hold of the land as the plains give
way to the mountains. Verdant grasses still cover the earth, only thinning as
they approach the white peaks of Erid Nimrais. Directly west, a wide, ancient
road makes its way towards the hills.
name: Rising Slope

desc:
Wide green meads flow for miles around. Slightly undulating plains stretch as
far as mortal eyes can see, forming a gap between the dark Misty Mountains in
the north, and the snow-capped White Mountains in the south. If the light is
favourable, Sir Angren can be seen, several miles west, cutting its wide scar
through the Gap of Rohan before turning to meet the sea. Directly south, a small
dirt trail winds a northeast to southwest path through the grassy lands.
name: Fields of Calenardhon

desc:
A small but heavily travelled dirt trail winds through the grassy plains of
Rohan. This path is riddled with deep horse tracks that cross back and forth
over each other, until the tracks become unreadable. Tall grass grows up on each
side of the trail, forming miniature walls of green that constantly threaten to
close in if the path is ever left unused. The trail continues south and east
before becoming lost from vision, hidden by the grass.
name: Eastern Trail

desc:
A narrow earthen path snakes a path though the plains of the Riddermark. The
path is only visible for a few miles in the west and north before the thick
grass swallows it from view. The trail itself is heavily used and barren of
foliage. Heavy, deep hoof prints cover the ground. The tracks cross back and
forth so many times that reading them becomes nearly impossible. On either side
of the trail, the verdant grass so common on these plains takes possession of
the land once more.
name: Eastern Trail

desc:
The grassy plains of Rohan gently rise and fall as they stretch towards the
horizon. Tall grey-green blades gently sway in even the gentlest breezes, giving
the land the appearance of a green sea of waves. To the west and north, a small
dirt trail cuts a windy path through the verdant grasses. In the east, the
plains seem to continue on without end. Farther south, the grasses appear
charred and trampled, but recovering from the abuse.
name: Verdant Fields

desc:
This land has been trampled and injured by some ancient battle. The thick
grasses so common in Rohan grow sporadically here. Large patches of barren earth
appear to be slowly shrinking though as the grasses begin to retake this area.
Occasionally, the broken hilt of a sword or shaft of an arrow can be seen
protruding from the ground. Whatever battle occurred here obviously loosed some
terrible evil upon the earth, as the fertile soil is only now beginning to
recover.
name: Ancient Battlefield

desc:
The grey-green blades of grass seem to spread out as far as to the horizon. The
land is nearly flat for miles in every direction, only slightly rising and
falling, as the untilled earth tends to do. The skyline is unbroken by trees or
structures for many miles. Where the earth and sky do meet, massive mountain
ranges stretch their tall peaks into the air. In the north, the dark peaks of
the Misty Mountains are almost always shrouded in a thick veil. In stark
contrast, the snow-capped peaks of the White Mountains in the distant south
climb up into the clouds.
name: Green Fields

desc:
The green grass grows tall and thick underfoot, carpeting the land in a sea of
waving green. The green seems to continue forever in every direction save the
west. There, a large, cobblestone road runs a southerly path into the heart of
Rohan. The road appears to be very old as many of the white stones used as
pavement have broken and scattered over a wide area. Some of the stones have
even been scattered miles away from their original placement.
name: Flowing Plains

desc:
The plains stretch for miles around before being cornered near the entrance of a
small village to the east and a large road to the west. This land remains
uncultivated, despite the presence of Rohan settlers. The grass is lush and
green, mixed with patches of clover and a few wild flowers. Near the edge of the
road, a few bushes have taken root, possibly the result of some overly eager
farmer.
name: Plains by the Road

desc:
The main Rohan thoroughfare runs a southeast to northwestern path from the heart
of Edoras, to the fords of the Isen. Ancient rocks form a pavement underfoot
that allows for swift travel, despite the less than perfect placement of the
cobblestones. The road continues to the north and south, before it bends again.
The eastern plains gently roll off towards the horizon - a sea of green waving
in the breeze.
name: The West Road

desc:
Flat, white, wide cobblestones line a southwestern path to the heart of Rohan.
Time and weather have been harsh, as the pavement shows wear. Some of the larger
stones have broken, and blades of grass spring up in the cracks. Despite time
and nature, the road does not lie in disrepair. Someone has quickly repositioned
the rocks near their original placement, giving the road a worn but traversable
feel.
name: The West Road

desc:
The green colour of the plains meets the dull white of a once great roadway a
short distance to the south and west. Lush blades of grass grow so thick, that
when they meet the cobblestones of the West Road, a small green wall is formed
that stands almost two feet tall. Under this deep carpet of green, insects buzz
about, and small animals scurry to and fro, making small waves in the plains.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
Grey-green grassy plains stretch out for miles to the southeast. The deep grass
is thick and springy, cushioning each footfall. To the north, a small dirt trail
leads to the northeast - farther into the Gap of Rohan. In the far south, the
cobblestone West Road winds a white, rocky path deeper into the heart of Rohan.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
A wide footpath bends to the west and north here as it cuts a brown path in the
verdant green grass. The plains of Calenardhon are covered with an uncommonly
strong and resilient grass. In order to wear down the grass to the bare earth,
nearly consistent traffic would have to walk this path. Upon the ground,
numerous tracks of men and horses cross back and forth over each other until
they become almost unintelligible.
name: Eastern Trail

desc:
A small dirt trail runs a windy course through the gently rolling plains of the
Riddermark. The earth underfoot is riddled with hoof prints that criss- cross
each other so many times that it is almost impossible to discern how many have
travelled this way. The path is only wide enough for perhaps two horses to walk
side by side. The edges of the trail are lined with small wildflowers that bloom
in vibrant purples and yellows in the summer.
name: Eastern Trail

desc:
The usually tall and vibrant fields of grass have been stunted and scorched in
large patches. In some places the grass grows almost as tall as two feet, but in
other spots the earth is barren and stained with blood. Charred areas run for a
few feet and then stop abruptly. The grass is trampled in a few large areas and
nearly untouched in others. These kinds of battle scars mark areas of unrest in
Rohan.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The grass-covered earth gently rolls throughout the Gap of Rohan, undulating
like timid waves on a calm sea. Between the dark peaks of the Misty Mountains
and the snow-capped ridges of Erid Nimrais, the grey-green plains open like a
long gulf between the opposing ranges. Only occasionally is the green
interrupted by small patches of purple heather or yellow buttercups.
name: Western Plains

desc:
Lush green grasses spread out in every direction, save east, where the Isen
flows quickly by. Far to the north, the dark peaks of the Misty Mountains rise
above the horizon, creating a jagged tear between the land and the sky.
Mirroring the dark peaks in the north, the snow-capped peaks of the White
Mountains pierce the sky in the distant south. Between these opposites,
grey-green grass flows for hundreds of miles.
name: East Bank

desc:
Near the banks of the Isen, patches of reeds as tall as a man spring up from the
earth, nearly blocking the view of the river to the west. Among the deep
thicket, small birds can be heard chirping, but are rarely seen. At the base of
the patches of reeds, thick verdant grass takes possession of the land again,
and spreads out for miles to the north, east and south.
name: East Bank

desc:
The rolling plains of Calenardhon are hemmed in by various features of the
landscape on the west, south and east. Mighty and swift, Sir Angren flows in the
west, blocking all travel in that direction with its steep, unconquerable banks.
To the east and south, a dirt trail bends and winds its way though the plains,
as it leads to a large camp farther south. In the ground, repeated horse tracks
lead off to the north, and back again south. Also, deep scars can occasionally
be seen in the grass, marking spots where earth was displaced for some reason.
name: Upon the Eastern Bank

desc:
This trail sees heavy use by both horses and men. The constant trampling of the
dirt has packed it so tightly, that even weeds cannot flourish. The path has not
suffered the use well however. Deep ruts, potholes and grooves await travellers
at every turn. Also, there are places where the earth has been stained a deep
reddish-brown - the colour of dried blood.
name: Eastbound Trail

desc:
A large Rohan encampment has been settled here. Numerous tents and campfires are
scattered across the plains. Repeated footfalls have worn dirt paths to and from
various tents. Several flags and markers enclose a long and narrow archery
range. A huge hole at the southern end of camp has the largest path leading to
and from it. It also emits a pungent odour of trash and decay. The West Road
runs along the camp's western edge and just beyond lies the Ford of Isen.
name: At a Rohirrim Camp

desc:
A large cobblestone road cuts a winding path though the plains of the
Riddermark. This road is an ancient pathway connecting the western and eastern
ends of Rohan. Time and nature have been unkind to this passage, as its pavement
is broken and scattered over a wide area. Swift repairs keep the road in working
order, even if it is not as smooth as it once was to ride or walk upon.
name: The West Road

desc:
Large, sun-bleached cobblestones wind away towards southern Rohan. This road is
wide enough to accommodate several horses riding side by side, or a large
caravan. The pavement has been repaired hastily with small round pebbles that do
not match the original stonework. The road is not as smooth as it once was, but
it is functional and easy to walk upon.
name: The West Road

desc:
Rolling plains cover the Westfold Vale with a deep and springy grey-green
blanket. Here at the northwestern end of Rohan however, two travelled roads meet
near the banks of the river Isen. To the west, a narrow dirt trail follows the
path of the river, as the river flows to the southwest. To the east, a large,
white cobblestone road runs an opposite diagonal course, towards the heart of
Rohan in the southeast. Between those two roads, a wedge of grassland grows
wider and wider to the south.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
As this dirt path leads away from the cobblestone road in the east, it follows
the river's bank to the west. The trail winds dangerously close to the edge of
the steep eastern bank of the Isen, making travel along it difficult. If the
deep tracks in the dirt are any indication, this trail is heavily travelled by
both man and beast.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
This narrow dirt trail splits off from the road north of here. Hoofprints dot
the main part of the muddy path, clear evidence of recent use by many horses.
The trail itself follows the path of the Isen further into the West-Fold. Gazing
westwards, the Isen can be viewed as it flows quickly by, whereas on the east
side on the trail, the West Road makes its way through the landscape.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
The occupants of this land have recently repaired the once broken cobblestone
road. Stones, bleached white by the sun, have been obviously rearranged from
their original positions in order to fill in old gaps in the pavement. Blades of
green grass poke up from in between the stones, but none are very tall. The road
continues southeast into Rohan.
name: The West Road

desc:
Just east of the Fords of Isen, the West Road lies along the edge of a military
encampment. The road is composed of broken white cobblestones, weathered and
worn by time. Someone maintains this road however, as the broken pieces have
been uprooted and rearranged to cut down the amount of potholes and ruts. The
troops camped east of here must use this road often. Numerous tracks of both
human and horse litter the earth.
name: Road at the East Bank

desc:
As the river turns west, its raging waters smash against the insurmountable
eastern bank. Large eddies and whirlpools have formed at the bend and suck down
most of the debris in the water. As small brush, weeds and even fish get sucked
into the vortex; they only stay under the surface for a few seconds, before
being catapulted back into the air. Atop the eastern bank, a worn dirt trail can
be seen skirting along the edge of the earth.
name: Bend in the River Isen

desc:
The eastern banks rise even farther above the river's surface as the current
runs southwards. The western back is still steep and difficult to traverse, but
does not climb as high as the eastern bank. Leaving the cold, clear water by way
of those grassy slopes would be near impossible. In the south, the river bends
towards two very large dirt mounds that appear to have been erected in the
middle of the river.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river flows swiftly between two steep, slippery slopes. The clear blue water
speeds to the south, and approaches two mounds of earth several miles away. Deep
beneath the rippling water's surface, schools of fish swim sometimes with,
sometimes against the current. Aquating plants wave in the undercurrent, fed by
the sun's light even down on the riverbed.
name: River Isen

desc:
Clear azure waters speed their way south to the Great Sea. Between the water's
rippling surface and the smooth rocks of the riverbed, a world of creatures swim
by. Small forests of plant life spurt up from the sandy, rocky mix at the bottom
of the Isen. Along the riverbanks, deep green bushes rise above grey- green
grasses, as more life flourishes in the saturated earth. A clearly defined road
sits atop the western bank.
name: River Isen

desc:
The trail bends to the north and west in its course through the Gap of Rohan.
Sounds of quickly moving water can be heard in the east as the Isen picks up
speed in its southerly course. Directly south, a small patch of land, only a few
miles long has been trapped between the trail and the river as the road veers
west and then south again. Just beyond that stretch of green, massive mounds of
earth rise above the plains, blocking the view.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
A lush variety of small bushes and shrubs grow along the western bank of the
Isen. The riverbank is extremely steep, an effect of the quickly flowing current
coming down from the mountains. Small, twisted roots are visible, as the earth
is pulled away from the banks little by little. To the west, a distinct dirt
road runs north and south.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The vast verdant plains of Rohan come to an abrupt halt. The Isen runs directly
east of here and then bends towards the southwest. The thick grass underfoot is
deep and lush, but it can not hide the huge ruts and holes that litter the land.
Some distance south, someone has dug up huge amounts of dirt, and built it back
up into a small fort on the river's edge. In the west, two very different roads
converge, and head towards the earthen fort in the south.
name: Western Plains

desc:
The cold, clear waters slow as the riverbanks bend to the west and the north.
The riverbed lies somewhere far beneath the river's surface, but can be seen as
clearly as if it were at one's fingertips. The banks are covered in thick,
grey-green grasses that remain permanently water-soaked. The greenery, combined
with the steep slopes of the banks, makes it nearly impossible to climb. But
just a bit south, a large eyot sits in the river, allowing land travellers to
ford the river and boaters or swimmers an escape route from the water.
name: Bend in the River Isen

desc:
An ancient white cobblestone road dives directly into the waters of Sir Angren,
forming one of two large fords. Smooth, pale stones have been piled here to
allow safe passage across the swiftly moving waters. Wide enough for a small
army to cross, this ford connects to a small island in the middle of the Isen.
Standing tall upon that eyot are two massive earthen forts with a wide road
running between them.
name: Fording the Isen

desc:
After the fords, the river picks up a more common pace, as it speeds through the
Gap of Rohan. The farther one moves south, the deeper the water gets, and the
steeper the banks become. It would be extremely difficult to traverse those
grassy, slick slopes. As the water continues its southerly flow, fish life
becomes far more abundant, particularly near the river's edge. It is easy to
note the variety of life swimming along in the Isen, as the waters are crystal
clear.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river picks up both speed and depth as it flows around a bend. The banks
begin to squeeze the river as they draw nearer to each other, which, despite the
bend, have caused an increase in water speed. As the banks close in, plant- life
on them springs up everywhere. It is difficult to discern even one small bit of
barren earth from those saturated embankments. Thick grasses grow so tall, that
their tops bend over and appear to drink from the water.
name: River Isen

desc:
The small but well used dirt trail runs along the Isen as it journeys south. The
earth is very packed, but ill cared for. Potholes await the careless traveller
with every step. Some holes have merged into large ruts that run the entire
breadth of the trail. The path originates a few miles northeast, where it can be
seen splitting off the Great South Road.
name: Trail to the West-march

desc:
As the river flows west, it picks up small debris from its banks and carries
them downstream. The cold, clear water swiftly pushes anything in its path
farther west and towards the sea. The river bed lies many feet beneath the
waters surface, but can be seen perfectly, as can the multitude of fish and
river life passing long with the current.
name: River Isen

desc:
Clear blue water rushes swiftly around a nearby bend in the river. The swift
current has cut deeply into the earth and formed very steep, slick banks.
Climbing up those banks would be extremely difficult, as they are nearly
vertical and covered with moist, thick grass. The water itself is cold and deep.
Even in the hottest summer months, the Isen is strangely cold. In the winter the
water's temperature becomes unbearable as the river continues to flow despite
the freezing cold.
name: River Isen

desc:
A grassy field is blocked in on the north, east and south sides. In the north,
the lush, green grass is gradually stamped out by the multitude of white stones
from the Great South Road. In the south and east, the river Isen swiftly flows
down from the Misty Mountains and heads towards the sea. This close to the
water, the western bank of the river is easily seen and traversed, but across
the swift and wide current, the east bank steeply rises - seemingly impossible
to climb.
name: Boxed-in Plains

desc:
The Great South Road bends to meet the Fords of Isen in the east. The edges of
this wide cobblestone road spread as they near the bank, expanding the width of
the road by more than double. The road clearly continues across the river by way
of two large fords and a small island. White, smooth stones lead to the water's
edge and then continue, forming an easy path across Sir Angren. Another way
across the water cannot be seen either up or down stream.
name: Road at the West Bank

desc:
This broken cobblestone road looks ancient when compared to the well-maintained
dirt road that splits off to the north. The ground directly underfoot is
littered with small white broken stones that resemble an extremely wide roadway.
Years of use, weather and nature have not been kind and the pavement has been
shattered and scattered over the grasslands. Despite the sad condition of the
stones, it is still fairly easy to determine the direction the road was built to
follow. To the southeast, the road meets the fords of Isen. In the northwest,
the Great South Road disappears into the tall grasses of Rohan.
name: Great South Road

desc:
A dirt trail, or perhaps a very narrow road, splits from the Great South Road
directly south. The once large but now broken cobblestones litter the earth for
miles, but this trail has been cleared of them. Small piles of white pebbles
collect on both sides of this dirt road. Long lines in the earth have been left
by the rocks as they were raked to the sides of the cleared dirt. To the
southeast, the Isen bends to flow around two massive earth mounds.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The trail begins to widen, as it leads farther south and narrows to the north.
Well travelled, this near road has no signs of weed or grass encroaching on its
borders. Repeated footfalls of two and four legged creatures have packed the
dirt so tightly that even the harshest rains have not caused damage. Also,
someone undoubtedly tends to the condition of this trail. Along its western
edge, small white pebbles litter the grass, but there are none on the ground
here. At the trails edges, small piles of pebbles appear to have been raked out
of the path by a very wide stick, or perhaps a large boot.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The road that leads to and from Isengard is a well-maintained dirt passageway.
Heavy traffic to and from the stronghold has packed the earth well, giving the
trail the ability to withstand even the harshest weather. The path of the
roadway bends here, turning east and south. Past the edge of the eastern bend,
the Isen can be seen, swiftly passing in its journey down from the Misty
Mountains, and towards the Great Sea. In the south, a wide patch of white rocks
can be spotted in the vibrant green grass.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
Deep green fields flow out in every direction. The nearly treeless landscape
offers few guides for the inexperienced traveller to follow. In the east
however, someone has seen fit to clear a large swath in the grass. A well-worn
road leads northwards, towards the dark peaks of the Misty Mountains. In the
south, the road intersects with a larger road in a horrid state of disrepair.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
Near the corner of a wide intersection, lush emerald grasses butt up to the edge
of two very different roads. In the east, a clear, well-kept dirt road leads in
a nearly straight northwards line. To the south, an ancient, broken cobblestone
road winds its way through the grasslands of Rohan. Hoofed animals have beaten
the grass down here; as many tracks can be seem in the earth. Small white
pebbles, once a part of the Great South Road, have been scattered far from the
road by the same hooves that trampled the grass.
name: Trampled Plains

desc:
The dilapidated roadway underfoot is only a shadow of its former self. It is
clear that in the distant past, this was a wide and well-travelled road. Even
now, the cobblestones that remain are still lain wide enough to accommodate
three horses and their riders side by side. The edges of the bleached white road
are hard to discern, as years of weather and use have broken and scattered the
pavement. Weeds have grown up from between the rocks and pushed even the largest
stones aside. The broken highway cuts a northwest to southeast path through the
Gap of Rohan.
name: Great South Road

desc:
As the Great South Road approaches Sir Angren to the southeast, small repaired
areas are noticeable in the cobblestone pavement. The original roadway was paved
in large white cobblestones but the hasty patchwork has been done in packed
earth, giving the roadway a mottled appearance. A few miles southeast, the road
leads towards two large earthen mounds that rise above the grassy green plains.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The open plains so common in Rohan are boxed in here on the north, east and
distant south. To the north, the Great South Road cuts a white, rocky scar
through the Gap of Rohan as is turns to meet the Isen, which blocks the plains
to the east. Where the road and the river meet, massive mounds of earth rise
above the plains, obviously man-made. The Isen runs south for several miles
before bending to the west, blocking off the plains in the south. In a land
characterized by vast, open, unending plains, this uncharacteristic
configuration of landmarks may draw more attention than other areas of Rohan.
name: Plains near the Road

desc:
Gently undulating grasslands stretch out to the west for many miles. In every
other direction the plains are boxed in by road or water. Sir Angren blocks all
south and easterly passage. The Great South Road must be crossed if one wishes
to travel north. This area sees heavy traffic from the local animal life as
evidenced by the numerous tracks in the earth leading towards the riverbanks.
All the tracks double back and cross over themselves dozens of times, indicating
repeated visits to this water source.
name: Boxed-in Plains

desc:
The grey-green grasses of the Rohan plains thicken as they reach the fertile
banks of the Isen. The river flows quickly as it travels southwest. The swift
stream has cut steep banks into the earth and the slopes quickly drop off, like
a miniature cliff. The banks continue to the west before turning south to follow
the river's course south.
name: Western Bank

desc:
Water runs cold and fast between the twists and turns of the river's banks. The
water is clear, blue and deep, making it easy to see the riverbed but impossible
to stay still long enough to discern many details. The occasional errant twig or
uprooted plant travels swiftly with the speeding current.
name: River Isen

desc:
As the river flows southwards, it gains momentum, splashing loudly against a few
large rocks that have popped up above the rippled surface. It is possible to see
the riverbed here as the waters run fairly clear, only marred by the occasional
twig or errant leaf. The waters seem to increase in speed as they approach a
small bend in the south.
name: River Isen

desc:
The fertile earth near the banks of the Isen overflow with lush green grass,
creating a slippery slope leading into the waters of the river. Even in the
driest seasons, the verdant grass grows so thick that the embankment becomes
extremely difficult to traverse. No bushes or trees offer any handholds on this
particular section of the river's edge.
name: River Bank

desc:
The earth slopes gently downwards and becomes slightly waterlogged towards the
south. The change in soil has caused a dramatic change in what was once the
greenery. Hundreds of wild flowers have taken root in the saturated dirt and
choked out the grass that is so prevalent in these plains. Multi-hued flower
heads sway in even the gentlest of breezes and grow several feet tall. The wild
flowers only last for about a mile before the grass reclaims its hold over the
land, but the change is dramatic enough to make this spot a small landmark on
the otherwise endless plains.
name: Slight Depression

desc:
Vast grassy plains stretch out for miles. The earth is covered in a deep sea of
green that waves in a strong breeze. Many miles south, reflections of light play
on the choppy surface of a mighty river. In the distant east, a wide road
creates an off-white path through the grass as it winds towards far off mountain
ranges in the north and south.
name: Green Plains

desc:
Small pebbles hidden in the almost impossibly tall grass threaten to undo any
sure-footed passer-by. The small stones have somehow been flung away from the
large cobblestone roadway in the north and east. The scattered stones could have
been moved by the hooves of wandering animals, as several horse tracks can be
seen in the dirt. If one were to take note of the surroundings anywhere but
underfoot, thick, green grass flows to the south for as far as the eye can see.
On a clear day, a slight glimmer can be seen, far in the southeast, as light
reflects off some water source.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A cobblestone roadway lies in ruins. Large, white, flat stones that once were
set close enough to stifle the verdant grass have been broken and worn into very
small, scattered pebbles. From where the rocks lie, it is safe to assume that
this road would have accommodated large caravans easily. However, the lush green
grass has now uprooted the pavement and scattered it over a larger area. Unless
one stands in the centre of the road, its direction is difficult to ascertain,
but from mid-road, it is clear that the road leads southeast and northwest.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The verdant grass abates to the south and is littered by small, white pebbles.
The cause of this change in the scenery is a wide, cobblestone road in a state
of remarkable disrepair that lies directly south. Where the rocks have snuffed
out the grass, small veracious weeds have sprung up, colouring the land with
touches of yellow, lavender and blue. As the road runs to the southeast, it
intersects with a small trail leading northward.
name: Pebbled Field

desc:
Huge mountains in the north and south border these plains, forming a large gap
in the landscape. This gap is covered with a thick, green carpet of lush grass,
unmarred by tree or rock for many miles. Small imperfections underfoot can be
seen through the grass. Rabbit holes litter the earth here, the result of a
family's nest. This makes mounted travel difficult, but not impossible.
name: Western Plains

desc:
Green, vibrant plains gently roll in the sporadic breeze. When the light
reflects off the grass just right, the land sparkles in rich golden tones that
seem to warm the earth. In the distant east, the odd sparkle of light shines off
the river Isen. Framing the grasslands to the north and south, vast mountain
ranges rise above the horizons.
name: Western Plains

desc:
Erratic rocks litter the land, interrupting the green grass with small, flat
patches of white. Farther south, the small stones on the ground become more
prevalent. The source of this change in scenery is an ill cared for cobblestone
road directly south. It is difficult to make out the direction the road takes at
this point, but it appears to be heading towards two small hills in the
southeast. Along what may have been the edge of the path, small colourful
flowers grow in abundance.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A once great road lies beneath your feet. Paved with cobblestones, this road,
when maintained, could easily have accommodated several riders on horseback.
Time has not been kind to this highway, and the flagstones that were once laid
in neat rows have been scattered over the land. From directly above the road, it
is still possible to discern the direction the pathway takes. Although it bends
as it leads away, the road clearly runs towards two small mounds of earth in the
southeast.
name: Great South Road

desc:
Thick vegetation extends southwards from the edge of a broken, paved roadway.
Several varieties of plant life have intertwined here to create a formidable
thicket. While not impenetrable, the brush does slow down any progress towards
the south. Northwards, the cobblestone road looks easier to traverse. A few
miles east, the road can be seen bending towards a raised area much farther to
the southeast.
name: Rough Brush

desc:
Nestled between mighty mountain ranges in the north and south, these plains make
up an area referred to as the Gap of Rohan. The snow-covered peaks of the White
Mountains lie far to the south, while in the north, the dark range of the Misty
Mountains tear jagged edges into the smooth horizon. This area, and the
surrounding plains, are characteristically covered in verdant, green grass.
Sometimes the grass could grow tall enough to tickle the underbelly of a pony,
whereas other times the grass is unnaturally short, as if grazed upon.
name: Gap of Rohan

desc:
Jade green grasses cover the land in every direction. Colourful wildflowers poke
their heads through the thick grassy carpet, breaking up the monotony. Hoof
prints can be seen spread throughout the grass, often near patches of grazed
down, barren earth. The tracks do not appear to be part of a very large herd,
but the general direction of all the tracks leads south and southeast.
name: Widespread Plains

desc:
Vibrant, lush grass-covered lands roll on for miles. Several miles in the
southeast, a large river throws off sparkles of light during the day and a muted
glow at night. As the plains approach the river, the earth falls away towards
the riverbank, and the grasses abate. Beyond the river, tall mountain peaks
tower many miles away.
name: Western Bank

desc:
The river Isen, running slow and steady, has cut a small ridge into the plains.
This has led to the creation of a steep sandy bank. The grass so common in the
Gap abates and gets choked by sand, as it grows closer to its life-giving water
source. Where dirt and sand still mix, a clear footpath has been worn into the
land. Repeated use of this bank as a trail along the river has formed a path
even the most naive tracker could follow.
name: Steep Western Bank

desc:
In times long past, the River Isen must have swept through this land, sweeping
its way southwards, ultimately towards the sea. Over the ages, however, the
course of the river has altered and a bend in the river has silted over, which
now leaves an isolated lake that still retains some water and life, only does
this occasionally refill when the river floods.
name: An Ancient Oxbow

desc:
The river Isen formed the ancient, hay meadow in the distant past. It was once
flooded every year, but now the river seems to have calmed quite a bit, leaving
the earth drier. The hay grows in short stalks. The rocky soil underneath causes
any rain water that does fall here to be absorbed quickly by the ground
underneath.
name: Ancient Flood Meadows

desc:
Short, yellow to a greenish-blue coloured grassland, devoid of trees and with
only the occasional, low scrub bush that extends across this landscape. The soil
here is very dry and rocky. The general slope of this land moves from flat to
gently undulating as it moves further south. To the east, the ground slopes very
gently into the Gap of Rohan.
name: Overlooking the Gap of Rohan

desc:
A small hill seems to have grown up out of the centre of the green grass that is
covering the ground. All around this tiny knoll, brightly coloured flowers can
be noticed rising up out of the verdant turf. From this vantage, one can see the
land sloping gently to the east, leading into the Gap of Rohan.
name: A Tiny Knoll

desc:
The grassland here seems to be an area of regrowth, having recovered from the
many travellers that spilled over from the road in ages past. There are some
natural flora of the area that has taken root once again, as rough scrub and
young saplings have begun to colonise the landscape.
name: Renewed Plains

desc:
When the Kingdoms of Gondor and Arnor thrived, this road must have bustled with
traffic and activity. With the fall of Arnor and the decline of Gondor, the road
has been neglected and seldom travelled. Originally, this road was built upon a
small embankment, rising to five feet above the surrounding plains. Over time,
however, these embankments have crumbled, leaving the road pockmarked and
uneven.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The vast, grassy plains roll seemingly endlessly here. To the south lies the
Great South Road that once connected the kingdoms of Gondor and Arnor. The
short, dry grass indicates that the plains here do not receive very much
precipitation. An occasional, dried blossom bears further testimony to this
fact.
name: Dry Grassy Plains

desc:
The flat steppes are covered with short grass. In some places, the hooves of the
herds of wild horses that roam these lands, have trampled the grass. Only a few,
randomly scattered bushes dare to brave the fierce winds. These plains seem to
stretch north as far as the eye can see.
name: Western Plains

desc:
A large surface of exposed rock rises up from the surface of the prevailing,
flat grassland. The surface of the stone looks as though it was etched and
marked by a sword or a knife. This is a site of some obvious, ancient religious
significance. This is evidenced by images of large, carved warriors returning
from a hunt, scored deeply into the exposed rock face, which is now covered with
deep green moss.
name: A Natural Dais

desc:
Dry rocks and pebbles, interrupted by patches of short tufted grasses and hardy
bushes, lie strewn across the uneven land. In the plains here, the majority of
the ground cover is bare earth, albeit with readily visible, scraped holes and
burrows that are quite frequent in its surface. These plains stretch out in all
directions, sloping very slightly up towards the west and broken by a roadway to
the south. To the north, the plains appear to be interrupted.
name: Plains by a Road

desc:
The Numenoreans that built this road provided it with a slight raised camber in
the centre. Any falling rain would flow to the sides of the road and wash down
the embankments to the surrounding plains. The surface of the road is now broken
and damaged. What remains of the road cuts a true path running east to west,
dissecting the plains surrounding it.
name: Great South Road

desc:
A recently disturbed mound of earth, topped by a ring of twelve, broken spears
lies nearby the road toward the south. Nestled amidst the long grasses of the
plains, the mound is largely covered by flat heavy stones, obviously they were
taken from the road to the north. The spears look like they have barely been
tarnished or corroded by the elements.
name: A Ring of Spears

desc:
The grasses and earth are churned, trampled down and littered with some broken
weapons and shattered pieces of armour. Some hoof prints mill back and forth
amongst booted footprints and spilt blood has stained the earth black, leading
one to believe a bloody battle took place here recently.
name: A Bloody Field

desc:
Meadow grasses and flowers have dried here through poor drainage and a lack of
water. What is left, is a natural hay field, which seems to have become quite a
favoured eating place for a variety of plant-eating grazers. Dung from the many
overfed animals appears to be everywhere, in various stages of moistness and
fragrance.
name: Dried-out Plains

desc:
The smooth, meadow grass and yellow dandelions grow a little more than several
boot-lengths in height and are well grazed. Dry, but fertile soil, accompanied
by a ready supply of fresh manure, has led to a rich variety of wildflower
growth, as well as the buzzing of various insects through the meadow.
name: Wildflower Meadow

desc:
Closely, cropped grasses cover this area. Barely an inch or two in height, they
carpet the gently undulating ground. Horse and cow droppings are common to find
amongst this landscape. There are a few, straggly bushes of gorse and smaller
shrubs that look distinctly nibbled upon.
name: Grazed Pasture

desc:
Much of this area of lowland grasses are covered with strange, low mounds of
dirt, covered in short grass and small trailing plants such as rockrose and wild
thyme. The ground seems alive with small movements, as several hundreds of tiny
ants go about their work.
name: A Sward of Anthills

desc:
Dusty, parched stems of long grasses move gently around on the wind, brittle and
tinder dry, in this area of stony, drained soil. Dried out bracken and heathers
await a spark or flame to cleanse the land and even the occasional small animal
that passes through seems to sense the tension.
name: Dried Grasses

desc:
There is almost no life here in the soil or on the ground. What does remain is
wilted, dried out for lack of moisture. The hardier strains of plants exist
further out on the fronds of land, forming a jagged oval of dead land. There are
no great signs of battle or any evidence of inhabitants.
name: Parched Earth

desc:
The tall grasses that are rising around this place, conceal a rich variety of
plants, other than those in plain view. Dandelions and daisies, rye grass and
thistle are all found here in quantity. In more secluded patches of growth,
purple and spotted orchids are poking forth.
name: Weeds and Orchids

desc:
The wild grasses of these plains have a distinctly flattened look, as if many
people spent time around here or camped on this spot for a long time. Grass
here, is more yellow then the rest and in several places, showing wear. Other
signs suggest the presence of old campfires.
name: Flattened Grasses

desc:
Broken stones and the occasional pothole make travel on this portion of road
hereafter treacherous. The embankment to the south of the road is crumbling and
falling apart. A portion of the road on that side has collapsed into it. A
small, rocky incline borders the road to the north. Falling rocks and some
stones from the slope, lie in a pile of debris by the edge of the road.
name: Great South Road

desc:
The plains that stretch out to the north are put asunder here by a small ramp as
the land falls by about fifteen feet. At the bottom of the incline lies the
Great South Road. There is almost no grass growing on the slope, leaving the
surface loose and crumbly and making the footing uncertain.
name: At a Slope

desc:
Downy and meadow oat grass predominantly covers these limestone-soiled plains.
These are interspersed with the occasional hawkweed patch, primarily in areas
where the soil is especially suited. The ground is soft and springy underfoot,
causing to travel through on foot a bit easier.
name: Lush Greenery

desc:
The land here undulates gently, covered in all directions with low grasses and
sedges. Small flowers can be seen dotting the surface of this green sea. The
ground seems to be soft underfoot, and appears to be a fine place to rest and
escape the cares of travelling. Towards the south, the ground slopes slowly
upwards, a slight break to the otherwise, flat terrain.
name: Flowing Grasses

desc:
The largely flattened landscape, typical of this region, is interrupted here by
a small rise almost like a dune, arguably a hill. From its peak, a little more
than thirty feet above the surrounding terrain, the course of the Great South
Road can be clearly seen. It curves southward to avoid the rise that runs along
the eastern edge.
name: A Small Hill

desc:
The road skirts around a small rise in the land towards the north. The
flagstones that originally constituted this road's surface are chapped and
crumbling from centuries of neglect. Flat plains stretch out to the south, while
the view to the north is obstructed by a small hill.
name: Great South Road

desc:
Long grasses that have spread away from the paved road to the north, stretch out
to the south in a haze of mixed yellows and greens. Travelling through the waist
high grasses seems strangely unreal, almost as if they are guiding those who
travel here across a tranquil sea, creating the illusion of the land drifting
idly by on a current of grass.
name: A Flat Plain

desc:
Long, bending grasses rise well above waist height, creating a vista of yellow
and green. In certain places, they rise even more, almost to a height of being
over ones head. Occasionally, a thicket of thistles makes travel a bit more
difficult. For the most part, however, the going is easy, for one with a good
sense of direction.
name: Long Grasses

desc:
Scorched, bare earth, barely colonized by a variety of small shoots of some type
of plant life, stretch for a mile or so in the midst of the grassland. The earth
looks dry and baked, with grasses at the edges of this blackened zone to look as
if they could burst into flames with the smallest spark.
name: Fired Earth

desc:
Tall grasses and invading heather battle for control of this area of the
landscape. Other plants and wild flowers such as ling, bedstraw and old man's
beard watch the battle for supremacy from their unobtrusive hiding places
amongst the stronger weeds, awaiting the outcome.
name: Heather Grassland

desc:
This ancient grassland, undisturbed by the plough or hoe, is growing here in
abundance. The slender grasses reach no higher than the toe of a boot or a shoe.
Harebell and cowslips grow amongst the grasses as well. There are also the
telltale signs of ant colonies, tufts of miniature hillocks of moss and matted
grasses.
name: Short Grasses

desc:
Grass for animal herds and yellow, oat grass dominates your view, with only the
occasional buttercup or other flower visible amongst the tall vegetation. To the
north, an invasion of heather and gorses looks to be imminent, but for now, the
grassland dominates the view, with some scrubs far to the north.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Heather, purple moor grass and bracken dominate this darkened area of heath,
although all the plants here look like seedlings. The soil is covered in old
ash, with some bleached and blackened bones peaking out from under the new
growths. It looks as though a ferocious inferno must have burnt here, quit some
time ago.
name: Heathland Scrub

desc:
A dried-up pond, now filled only with dust and dying plant life, lies in a low
dip amidst the wilting grasses. The pond seems to have no obvious source of
refilling, leading to the assumption that it may only fill through some heavy
rainfall. Whatever the cause, there is little here to sustain life.
name: A Dry Pond

desc:
The stones underfoot bear a strong resemblance to the foundation of a stables
complex, with room for several horses and possibly even a hay wagon or two, or
what remains of them. The occasional beam marks out where the stalls once stood.
Everywhere, invading plant life is reclaiming the land. A large, stone water
trough is lining the outside wall.
name: Ruins of a Stable

desc:
A very decayed, yet once sturdy waystation, built in the classic Dunadan style
lies here, ruined with its interior exposed to the elements. The house seems to
have been unoccupied for many years and its surrounding wall and hedge are in a
state of disrepair and chaos. A rusty gate leads to the road just north, while
the crumbled remains of the station wall keep the ancient garden separate from
the great plains of the Dunlands. This waystation probably housed many visitors
when the Great South Road was first built.
name: At a Ruined Waystation

desc:
A thick layer of leached soil covers much of the road, as it continues on to the
east and the north. Buried amongst the scrub vegetation that grows wildly here,
is a broken wheel from some long gone wagon or carriage. It lies abandoned and
rotting, a sign of a bygone age, reminding travellers this road was once a major
route of commerce between two mighty and prosperous kingdoms. Along the southern
road-side an old hedge-row grows, blocking all passage, except for an opening to
the south, guarded by a rusty old iron gate.
name: Bend in the Great South Road

desc:
The road curves from west to south here, avoiding a small hill directly to the
east. Completely overgrown with brush and scrubs, some portions of the road's
stonework are only occasionally visible, broken and posing more of a hazard than
an aid to those wishing to travel over. The better used section of road along
the northern edge testifies to the more popular route.
name: A Curving Decrepit Road

desc:
The absence of water has made the ground a little more than dry and sun-cracked.
All but the most hardy of plant life has perished, leaving in the remaining
desolation none but the more waxy, spiny plants that have adapted to such arid
conditions. This lack of water extends further to the north, where the land is
all but dead.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
The ground dips here, forming a slight depression that is no more than thirty
feet across and perhaps five feet deep. Its slopes are lined with arrow grass
and other damp-loving plant life. All the water to be found, is in the centre of
this area, in the form of a small puddle, muddy and choked with a lot of
pondweeds. To the west there is a strange rock, barely visible off in the
distance.
name: A Dry Water Hole

desc:
The remains of the Great South Road lie here, marking an east-west trail as the
great road once did. In places, the road is in remarkable condition, especially
since it hasn't been maintained for a long time. The short, stubble grass on
either side of the road offers an inviting place to rest. The terrain is flat
and the road stretches towards the horizon in both directions.
name: Great South Road

desc:
Cudweed and knotgrass appear to grow abundantly amongst forget-me-nots, poppies
and speedwells. Nearby, a great road bends to the north and east but all passage
to and from it is blocked by an ancient hedge-row. As one gets further from the
road, the grasses grow taller and overtake the smaller, but less competitive
plants. To the south, there seems to be a small opening in the tall grass.
name: Weeds and Wildflowers

desc:
A carefully, paved path passes east to west from the ruins of an extremely old
looking house, to what seems to be the remnants of a once beautiful garden. The
path is difficult to see until stumbled upon, as grasses and a few, tall,
scraggly bushes provide almost total seclusion.
name: An Overgrown Path

desc:
Wild primroses and cowslips grow plentifully amongst the undisturbed, wild
grasses. The soil is damp and the vegetation is lush and vibrant. There is a
tiny spring trickling up from the earth, but is quickly absorbed by the
surrounding soil, making it unusable as a water source.
name: Wild Grasses

desc:
A combination of rather unpromising soil conditions and the lack of wind
breaking the vegetation, has led to the development of heath land here. Heather
and gorse dominate the plant life, choking down the tall grasses that are
present elsewhere in the region.
name: Scraggly Heathland

desc:
The rolling grassland gives way to an area of heath here, showing signs of fire
consuming it not too long ago, possibly to make way for farming. New shoots of
grass are peaking out from the ashes of the previous, flora-look green and
vibrant. The grass in many places obviously seemed a tasty treat for some
animals.
name: Blackened Vegetation

desc:
This area is full of wild nettles that are interspersed with the rolling grasses
that rise above waist height. Ground-nesting birds and a few other small animals
are visible in the brush. The ground is soft and damp to touch, hinting at an
underground water source, tantalizingly out of reach.
name: Wild Nettles

desc:
An area of hawthorn scrubs have forced its way in amongst the tall grasses,
thriving on the dry, well-drained soil that is prevalent in these plains. The
ground under and around the hawthorn bushes is carpeted in dog rose and
brambles. Perhaps, in several years from now, taller trees will start to
develop.
name: Hawthorn Scrub

desc:
A dry, hard mud trail wends its way through the long, surrounding grasses. The
ground seems well-drained and generally dry. Purple fescue grasses grow along
