the edges of the trail. Here and there, signs of grazing by livestock and horses
are visible. The trail continues west and north.
name: A Hunting Trail

desc:
A faint trail either begins, or perhaps come to an end here, leading to the
south through medium to tall grasses and shrubs. The path is very faint and it
is hard to tell whether the trail is caused by people, or perhaps being the work
of animals. The sides of it are covered by tall shrubs and some scraggly plants.
name: A Faint Trail

desc:
The rolling, grassy plains around this region have recently been disturbed, as
evidenced by a number of freshly excavated holes and patches of newly turned
earth. The cause of this disruption is not immediately evident, as there are no
signs of any man-made cause.
name: Disturbed Ground

desc:
An overgrown garden, full of barley, oats and buckwheat has been left here, but
barely visible amongst the tall grasses that have invaded its boundaries from
without. There are no signs remaining here of any dwelling that may have once
accompanied the garden. Within a few more years perhaps the remaining signs of
the garden itself will also be erased.
name: An Overgrown Garden

desc:
Thistle, cornflower and poppies stand out amongst the one to two feet tall, wild
grasses that populate this area. The grass is teeming with some small wildlife
and is rarely silent, constantly disturbed by the movement of the wind through
the stalks and leaves, along with the chattering of the hidden animals and
insects.
name: Wild Meadows

desc:
The stone used to build this road was hauled here from quarries long distances
away. Running in an east-west direction here, the remains of the Great South
Road cut through the vast plains that constitute the Gap of Rohan. There are
short grasses that cover much of the road and the surrounding plains. To the
north there is a large rock that is barely visible. South of the road, an old
hedge-row grows, making passage into the open meadows quite hard, if not
impossible.
name: Great South Road

desc:
A huge, black boulder rests partially embedded into the ground, some fifteen
feet high and nearly thirty feet across. It is surrounded by many, smaller
rocks, all strewn about with no apparent meaning to their arrangement. The rocks
give a refreshing break from the monotony of the flat landscape that is
surrounding them.
name: A Rock by the Road

desc:
The grass that once grew here has dried from lack of water. In some places, what
remained of the grass has been eaten or trampled by animals. South of here lies
the ancient road that once ran between the Kingdoms of Gondor and Arnor. Far
away to the north the Misty Mountains are visible, climbing imposingly out of
the prairie.
name: A Field of Straw

desc:
Buried underneath the dust and weeds, one can discern the remains of what was
once a mighty road. On either sides of the road lie vast, windswept plains.
Hampered by the flagstones concealed underneath, the path of the road is largely
devoid of any thick vegetation and presents a fairly negotiable path to
travellers. South of the road, an old and deep drainage dike is replaced by a
hedge-row further to the east, blocking passage into the open southern plains.
name: Remains of a Road

desc:
From deep green to an almost blue, a sea of grass seems to swell and ebb all
about like the tide of an ocean. The variety of different grasses all blend
together to form an endless pattern in all directions. To the north, on the
other side of an ancient, drainage canal are the remains of the Great South Road
that runs east and west continuing its ancient journey across the Dunlands.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
Several varieties of sweet smelling shrubs, combine with verdant grasses, to
create a heady scent that is overpowering this grassy plain. A vast variety of
rich plant life, overflows with only the occasional cropped spot, due to obvious
grazing. The shrubs grow denser towards the east.
name: Scented Grasses

desc:
The grassland forms a dense turf, much shorter than in the surrounding lands.
The soil is well drained and dry. The grass is replaced here and there, with
mosses and a variety of fungi. Stands of various types seem to be widespread and
a number of other types of plants appear to grow hardily from old horse dung,
which is plentiful here.
name: Downland Turf

desc:
Medium length grasses, interspersed with buttercups and cowslips, cover much of
this area. Many of the plants look well nibbled upon. Much of the grass has been
well grazed, often down to the base and many plants have made use of the rich
soil provided through an abundance of horse dung.
name: Well Grazed Grassland

desc:
A slightly worn path appears out of the plains here and continues to the north,
where it intersects with other trails. The signs of disuse are clearly visible
by the number of weeds growing on the edge of the trampled ground. When the
local inhabitants ceased using it, the verdant green grasses quickly reclaimed
the plains.
name: Disused Path

desc:
Several small, worn trails criss-cross here, although south and east they appear
to be less used. To the north, one can barely see a ridge in the plains, while
to the west lies the southern skirt of the Misty Mountains. The only signs of
life are the countless hoof-marks that pepper the mud of the trail. It would
appear that recently a great number of beasts were driven or ridden along it.
name: Intersection of the Trails

desc:
Cutting its way across the plains, this trail is but one of many created by the
movements of beasts and hunters. Hoof marks are easily visible in the soft turf,
often clumped together in areas near the track. To the north are the burnt
remnants of a village below a rising ridgeline, and beyond it on the horizon are
the southernmost foothills of the Misty Mountains, Dol Baran.
name: Small Trail

desc:
The high grass nearby gives way to a clearing, burnt by fire that appears to
have been fairly recent. A few remnants of wooden buildings still remain,
scorched and blackened. It is impossible to tell who lived here, but with the
constant wars between Rohirrim, Dunlendings and orcs in this area, such raids
are commonplace. A ridge rises above the plains to the north and east, while to
the south the flatlands continue as far as the eye can see.
name: Burnt Area below a Ridge

desc:
A ridge runs for miles along the plains, a little more than a small scree here.
Plants and shrubs of many different varieties grow atop it, giving way to short
lush turf elsewhere. The ridge continues southeast across the plains, becoming
steeper in the distance. To the east, grasses and scraggly bushes grow densely.
name: Atop a Ridge in the Plains

desc:
The orderly nature of the plains ceases here, and a dense green thicket of
undergrowth sprouts up in a sudden depression. The dark vegetation is a harsh
contrast to the otherwise light shades of plains to the west. Water from the
densely vegetated thorns, which stretch out just east and north, must drain to
here, for the base of the depression is slightly boggy in parts.
name: A Thick Clump of Vegetation

desc:
The grass in this area is little more than a browned coverlet, partly due to the
sun and the stony soil. North, the plains resume their normal covering of
springy turf, whilst south the ridge falls fairly steep down, offering a climb
of about a dozen yards.
name: Crest of a Ridge

desc:
Underneath a large, rocky protrusion in the plains, this area has been well
sheltered from the winds which sweep across the Gap of Rohan. The grass has
responded well to this respite, growing to the height of a man's waist in parts.
Dense growth makes it hard to see, and countless boulders that have fallen from
the ridge above, make it hard to progress beyond this place.
name: Below a Steep Ridge

desc:
This small path leads to the rotten remains of a farmstead just south of here,
now being little used, since its owners are long gone. In places the trail all
but disappears, only to pop up again a few dozen yards away. There is a large
ridge that cuts across the plains to the northeast, blocking passage due to its
stony sides.
name: Remnants of a Trail

desc:
The natural grass and plant life has taken over what was once an orderly row of
vegetables and grains. A few parts of the former crop grow here, but now very
sparse among the lush plains of grass. There is no sign of a farm house, but
parts of broken farm implements lie upon the ground, abandoned some time ago.
Beyond the farm remains, lies a thicket growing out of a depression in the
earth, which blocks further travel southward.
name: Overgrown Farmland

desc:
The river here is broad and slow, not to regain its mountain swiftness while in
the flat lands of the Gap. The broken plains on the eastern bank are the only
view of the land of Rohan, while to the north the giant presence of the Misty
Mountains dominate the horizon. The river downstream enters a broad valley, its
eastern bank beginning to grow steep.
name: River Isen

desc:
Many, small boulders and underwater rock formations would have made this a
dangerous part of the river, but the current is not quick enough here to pose
any hazard. The water ramps over and around the smooth-sided rocks in many
places, forming many whirls and small side currents. On its west bank runs a
road at the base of a steep ridge, while to the east the riverbank slopes
steeply towards broken plains.
name: River Isen

desc:
A low shoulder of rock allows passage to the west bank, where the road bends
away from the river to avoid flooding. The Isen is wide and slow here, with a
steep slope of half a dozen yards on its eastern side. To the south, the valley
through which it runs opens up into the flat lands of the Gap, where miles upon
miles of soft turf stretch out in the distance.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river begins to run calm here, as it widens during its flow southward. The
water becomes shallow near the banks, with the smooth algae-covered bed that is
clearly visible a few feet down. A tall shoulder of steep rock prevents any
passage to the eastern bank, while on the western side there are rolling folds
that are so typical in the Gap of Rohan. A small, water channel runs through
them, and empties its trickle of water into the river.
name: River Isen

desc:
The river is wider here than at any other visible point, flooding out into the
reedy banks to the west. The eastern bank is steeper, although it has eroded
into a muddy and slippery slope. The water below is still deep though, easily
deep enough to prevent any chance of fording even at its lowest level. The stony
river bed is only visible near the banks, while in the central channel the only
thing noticeable underwater is the occasional darting fish.
name: River Isen

desc:
Waist high grass, coloured somewhere between green and brown, almost covers
these riverside fields completely. Travel is somewhat slow because of this. It
gets even worse to the east, where the land grows very soggy as it nears the
waters of the Isen. Westwards, the road to Isengard runs by on slightly higher
ground, avoiding any chance of flooding.
name: Fields by the River Isen

desc:
Rolling down in gentle folds of land from the road to the west, these plains are
simply miles of soft, upland turf. An occasional clump of reeds or low bushes
lies nearer to the river, but the grass dominates. Flowing down from the road is
a small line of water, in an almost perfectly straight channel. This connects to
the gutters of the old highway, and keeps it free of any flooding even during
heavy rains.
name: Riverside Fields

desc:
Unpaved, but flat and smooth, this broad highway was cut by the men of Numenor
in ages past, linking the fortress of Isengard to the Fords of Isen. It is still
maintained today, so that armies can sweep down to quickly block the Gap of
Rohan. It follows the line of the Isen for the most part. Further south, it
diverges by a few miles, taking a more direct route.
name: Curve in the Road to Isengard

desc:
The ancient roadway runs its course north to south on a narrow strip of land
between the steep, valley slopes and the river. It is a wide road, made for
swift passage, so travel is easy and unimpeded. South, the valley opens up into
the plains of the Gap of Rohan, while to the north the road and river head
towards the mouth of Nan Curunir. The valley lies in a cleft at the end of the
Misty Mountains with its countless vast peaks rising on either side.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
This part of the road passes under a pebbled slope on its western side, which
grows to a ridge further south. The highway itself is in order, and has seen
much work of late. Near the riverside, large wooden props now support the edge
of the roadway, which was being eroded by the waters.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The ridgeline diminishes from its mighty height south-west to a mere slope here
over the road. Slightly treacherous because of the loose stones, it nevertheless
seems to be an easy route down. Underfoot the turf is soft and springy, as it is
for miles around to the west and north. The only interruption to the plains are
the peaks of the Misty Mountains, which rise into the clouds not many miles
north of here.
name: End of the Ridgeline

desc:
The steep, western slopes of the valley runs across the plains westwards from
here, a great ridgeline blocking travel for many miles. It would be possible to
climb down eastwards, although not without risk, while to the south, the slope
is too steep to descend. Below, the road to Isengard bends around the feet of
the ridge, and continues on in a north-south direction. The river Isen itself
lies about two miles east, a wide waterway marking the western borders of Rohan.
name: Overlooking the Valley of the Isen

desc:
Coming up against the steep slopes of the valley, the road bends away east,
nearer to the river Isen. Underfoot, it is a wide highway, being unpaved but
smooth and hard-packed. It was built so that a large army could ride swiftly
down from Isengard to block the Fords of Isen, and for that purpose it is still
kept in good order. Westwards, a long running ridge overshadows a fertile
valley, thriving in its shelter, while south the road continues along the
western side of the valley.
name: Bend in the Road to Isengard

desc:
The road travels under the shadow of a ridge on the western edge, which reaches
its highest point nearby. Between it and the road, there is a small line of
water that runs down a gutter, cutting under the road in a narrow, man-made
tunnel and heads towards the river. Such water channels were built long ago by
the Numenoreans to prevent flooding of vital military highways, and have been
maintained by the Rohirrim and the guardians of Isengard ever since.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
Tracks of men and horses are scattered on the road, which runs north to south in
the valley of the Isen here. The Isen River runs only a few miles to the east,
its sparkling mountain waters are easily visible. To the west lies the pebbled,
western slope leading up to a ridge, where a scree of stones appears to be
treacherous underfoot. It would be easier to ascend south of here, where the
slope is less steep.
name: Road to Isengard

desc:
The ridge here is a little more than a grassy scree of countless small stones,
often rolling down to the base of the wide road below. To the west, the grass
wins its battle with the pebbles, and stretches for many miles into the turf of
southern Dunland. East, the River Isen lies just a mile or so beyond the road,
marking the western border of Rohan.
name: Pebbled Slope above the Road

desc:
A small ridge forms here, overlooking the road and the River Isen below. To the
west, the plains continue as rolling grasslands through the Gap of Rohan, while
to the north the land dips below another ridge into a lush valley. That would
probably prove the easiest route to the road below, as the slope here would need
careful climbing to descend.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
Overshadowed by the ridge to the north, the grasslands here have become lush in
their sheltered valley. Once a mostly deserted land due to the wars of orcs and
men, now wildlife flourishes from the rich earth. All around, the valley plants
and shrubs sprout from the ground, well nourished by the nearby waters of the
Isen. Just before the river to the east lies a wide road, following the line of
the water north and south.
name: Fertile Valley beneath the Ridge

desc:
The ridge continues on, running east to west, cutting across the plains below.
The ground is somewhat solid, but rocky in parts as the exposed ridge pokes
through the soil. The ridgeline diminishes a little to the east as it edges
towards the valley of the Isen, while to the west there is a narrow and very
knife-like outcrop that thrusts out from the rock.
name: Atop a Running Ridgeline

desc:
Rolling sedately along, the grasslands reach a peak here and begin to fall away
gently to the south and east. Eventually the land there drops down a ridge, a
line that splits the plains for miles. Beyond that the plains continues north up
to the mountains as miles, upon miles, of soft, springy turf.
name: High Point in the Grasslands

desc:
Walls of thorns and shrubs seem to grow thickly all around here, in particular
to the south, where the tangle of vegetation is extremely dense. All of the
surrounding greenery turns dismally dark to the north, where a valley of dark
green grass cowers below a ridge.
name: Thick Vegetation

desc:
The plains here are spread across a large ridge, which dips down noticeably to
the south. This part is the highest for miles around, which exposes it to the
winds that whistle through the Gap of Rohan. However, the grass that is
underfoot, does not seem to have suffered. It is lush and soft everywhere.
name: Elevated Plains

desc:
Thrusting outward from the ridge that splits the plains, this outcrop is a thin
knife-like sliver of land. On all sides, the descent is treacherous, as the
eroded rock is not very stable. To the north, the plains appear to rise
somewhat. Noticeable beyond the plains there, the southern end of the Misty
Mountains rises forcefully.
name: An Outcrop from the Ridge

desc:
The ridge to the north is a knife-like outcrop, appearing to be near impossible
to climb and seemingly in danger of sudden collapse. Here, under its height,
lies a large, fertile valley. It runs through various shades of green as it
stretches out away from the ridge. To the west, some sort of trail is barely
visible in the distance.
name: In the Shadow of a Ridge

desc:
The field is covered in grass about one foot high, with some small shrubs
growing on the outlying area of the ridge to the north. Small rodents and game
birds thrive here, quickly scurrying to take cover at the scent of any large
animal or other threatening disturbances.
name: Grassy Plain

desc:
Covered in relentlessly waving beds of grass, this sheltered area lies in the
angle of a bending ridge. To the north it is steep overhead, perhaps half a
dozen yards or so, while to the east the ridge is merely a grassy slope that
appears to be inaccessible from this side. Directly above, it seems possible to
climb up out of this area.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
This high ridge rises slowly from the green plains and runs for several miles
north of here. Below, the river Isen runs by in its valley, with what appears to
be a road barely visible on the far bank. The vegetation on this ridge is poor,
in many places nothing but a light, mossy covering for the rough stone beneath.
To the south, the plains continues, just below the ridge. To the east, the ridge
slopes downwards.
name: Sloping Ridge

desc:
The stony soil here can support little more than the sparse vegetation, which
grows between the countless, rocky outcrops that dot this ridgeline. Near the
edge of the ridge, the ground is not very stable and appears that it would be
easy to trip on a crumbling chunk of rock. A steep slope leads down to the
riverside to the west, which is nothing more than a small chunk of broken plains
before the water itself.
name: A Broken Ridgeline

desc:
This elevated ridge, runs in a giant curve through the plains, enclosing the
valley of the Isen on its western side. This side of the river is mostly a
broken land, with the crumbled potholes common in the stony soil that cause any
mounted travel nearly impossible. Below and to the west, are some rocky plains
that expand over a few hundred yards. Even further away the Isen can be noticed
as well.
name: Along the Ridge

desc:
The ridge above is steep for a dozen yards or so, with a small and shallow
valley forming at its base. The ground here is rich and damp, with small, low
bushes covering much of it. The River Isen flows by to the northwest of here,
bending away from the far bank. The ridge to the east is a rocky and sheer
outcrop, although climbing straight up seems possible.
name: Valley below the Ridge

desc:
The river continues its flow north and west here, through the flatlands. There
is a well-kept road that runs parallel to the river on its western side,
elevated slightly by the earthen embankments. On the eastern bank, there are
broken plains, scattered with fissures and cracks in the ground. Far to the
northeast, the peaks of the Misty Mountains are visible in the distance as is
the Forest of Fangorn.
name: River Isen

desc:
The rough ground here is hard to travel, with many rocks and stones kicking up
underfoot. Large holes can also be seen all around the plains, all appearing to
be deep and seemingly impossible to climb back up from. A few miles to the east,
lies the eastern walls of the valley, too steep to climb though. Below, a
slippery slope leads down to the river Isen.
name: Broken Plains

desc:
These stony plains are stuck between the high, eastern, valley walls and the
river Isen to the west. A steep slope leads into the water, while the climb up
to the high ridge that is to the east, looks impossible. The landscape is very
uneven and hard to travel due to large rocks, boulders and some deep holes. It
seems to get even worse to the north.
name: Narrow Strip of Land by the River

desc:
This large, rolling ridge that lies just east of the rivern Isen, has blocked
any passage west. The plains here are similar to the lands southwards, flat and
green with a few rocks scattered on the ground. The land to the north seems less
hospitable and harder to travel through.
name: Broken Plains above the River

desc:
The River Isen provides a small variety to the otherwise unending plains. While
the usual vegetation of plains consists of grass only, closer to the river are
bushes that brave the harsh, stony soil and thrive off all the nourishment that
the river water provides. Hoof prints can be seen in the dusty earth, some of
them quite fresh.
name: Plains by the River

desc:
The interior of Dol Baran seems to have been hollowed out some time ago. One can
only speculate as to the original purpose, perhaps to lie in wait for enemies.
The walls are rough, as if carved by some blunt instruments. Wooden beams were
built against the walls and ceiling as supports. A layer of dust covers the
ground, making it almost impossible to discern how long it has been since anyone
has been through here. To the west, a passage leads off into darkness.
name: Inside the Hill

desc:
The support beams that lie to the east comes to an end in this section of the
tunnel. The walls become gradually smoother from the east to the west. The
ceiling is sloped along the contours of the hill above. There is no indication
as to who may have built this place. A few bits of metal lies scattered about
the ground, along with the occasional rock. The tunnel is very quiet, although
sound would travel very easily through it.
name: Smooth Passage

desc:
The surface of the passage walls is smooth and cool. Some sort of wall hanging,
with faded dyes, has been suspended on the southern wall. It seems as if this
was once inhabited. A small table lies along the western wall, but broken in
many pieces. Faded papers are strewn all around the floor, but they are
completely illegible, the ink must have faded long ago.
name: A Side Passage

desc:
The ceiling in the cave is tall and rocks that lie scattered around are blocking
the passage. Piles of bones and rotting corpses are on the ground, perhaps the
remains of previous inhabitants. No farther travel is possible, because several
large boulders, stones and other fallen debris line up on all sides.
name: Bird Cave

desc:
A handful of feathers are strewn upon the floor of this section of cavern, which
is wider than the area to the south. The walls here lack the scars of claw marks
visible to the south, as if whatever creature or creatures pass through here
either took more care or had more room to manoeuvre. Towards the north, what
might be a wooden support beam can be seen stretching high above.
name: A Widening Ingress

desc:
A large spool of thick, braided cord stands prominent against the western wall.
Behind the spool, there seems to be a few yards of sturdy leather, which is
marked to be cut and sewn. There are clearly wooden rafters, crossing the width
of this place, only a few feet overhead. Some small feathers that might have
covered the floor sit in a pile, nearby an old straw broom.
name: A Small Cavern

desc:
The walls of this section of the tunnel are quite smooth unlike the adjacent
caves. To the east, the ground slopes upwards towards a curve in the cave. The
air is still and cool, seemingly undisturbed for some time. It appears as though
any sounds would travel easily in here. Strange drawings mark the tough rocky
wall to the south.
name: Smoothly Worn Tunnel

desc:
The roughly-shaped walls run smoother to the west. It seems as though the bridge
between the entrance, from the plains to the south, was added much later than
the walls that run west. They are both cool and dry, unlike the slick, rough
rock to the south. The ceiling overhead is low, but does not hamper progress.
The ground slopes downwards to the west.
name: Transition

desc:
This small, side cavern is clearly man-made. The walls are rough, yet regular in
the way it has been carved out of the side of the tunnel. Not much more than a
place to put a small bed and desk, as this seems to be the resting place for
whoever inhabits these tunnels.
name: Small Side Cavern

desc:
The interior of this dwelling, though seemingly crude and squalid from without,
is well furnished and decorated. The walls are of stone and timber, with the
existing furniture being of carved wood and bronze. Some hand painted friezes of
a clan history decorate the walls and the floor is covered in warm animal furs.
name: Chieftain's Hut

desc:
This room consists of a little more than a cool and dry, solidly built hut,
containing storage bins, barrels and shelves of useful stores for the hard
winter months. The walls here are plain and undecorated, and the floor is of
clean looking straw.
name: Winter Stores

desc:
The ground underfoot is hard, packed earth, trampled by generations of clansmen.
The dwellings inside the palisade are all squat, windowless, round huts with
roofs made of hides and stone, with timber walls. Northward, there is a durable
and well cared for hut, looking as though it belongs to someone of importance.
To the south, the sturdy, low roofed, but somewhat run down, barracks seem to
have seen better days.
name: Inside a Dobac Hold

desc:
The dwellings here, within the timber, palisade walls, seem to be of the typical
Dunlending style. The houses are round and lack windows, with hide roofs with an
opening in the centre which allows smoke from cooking fires to escape. There is
a building to the north that has no such opening and has huge stacks of firewood
piled up against the walls, while the smoky hut to the south looks large enough
to be lived in by several families.
name: Inside a Dobac Hold

desc:
This vast and round, timber-walled hut contains thirty to forty woven sleeping
mats. The focal point of the room is a smoking fireplace inside a shallow pit,
which is surrounded by blackened stones. Some of the smoke from the fire escapes
by a hole in the thatched roof high above, but almost as much seems to try to
escape through the rest of the roof, the walls and the woven door.
name: A Thatched Smokehouse

desc:
The lack of natural light inside these barracks lends a certain degree of
dinginess to the interior. Woven, sleeping mats are scattered around the floor
and several, crude obscenities carved into the wooden walls give ample evidence
that this is a building inhabited by men, as if the smell alone were not enough.
name: Run-Down Barracks

desc:
Just below the wagon, a large cavity has been dug out, creating a very large,
termite's nest. They feed on the remnants of the rotting and decaying wagon
together. The cavity itself is irregular and crude, clearly made by animals and
littered with droppings, along with small carcasses and bones.
name: Below the Wagon

desc:
The deeper part of the basement lies in an eternal darkness. Most of the cellar
has avoided destruction, but the decay is still a prominent feature of the room.
Rotting shelves, barrels and containers litter the floor, and if there was
anything of value left when the inhabitants left, it has vanished long ago.
name: Ancient Cellar

desc:
The old cellar is in a state of decay, its walls crumbling from old age and from
sturdy roots pushing into the small cracks. Large blocks of stone have fallen
from above, crushing much of what was once here. Cracks and openings in the
ceiling allow a few rays of light to pierce the darkness at day, but fail to
illuminate much of the basement.
name: Ancient Cellar

desc:
The walls of the crypt are covered with large, flat stones. Along them, three
small recesses have been cut out to hold the remains of fallen warriors. In the
middle of the room, a slender stone pillar emerges from the earthy soil. The
pillar has obviously been here for ages because it is cracked, with some
fragments that have fallen off. The entire chamber appears to have been built
around the pillar.
name: Under the Mound

desc:
More than ten feet above the battlements, this watchtower provides a perfect,
unobstructed view of the land for many miles. The tower is large enough to allow
for a lookout and for a well-armoured man to comfortably draw a bow. Thick
leather strips are tied about the tower's posts to protect anyone from falling.
A small lantern hangs from a rope attached to the tower's slanted roof.
name: North Tower

desc:
Roughly ten feet above the ground, a narrow walkway has been constructed near
the top of the fort walls. This thin platform is supported with wooden spikes
that continue another ten feet into the air, forming the parapets of the
battlements. Sturdy ladders lead down from the battlements and up to a small
watchtower. A vibrant green flag with the image of a white stallion flies atop
the tower.
name: Northern Battlements

desc:
A large campaign tent has been erected adjacent to the mess hall. A flimsy
wooden table stands near the southern wall of the tent, covered in maps and
small position markers. Upon the earth, several flat planks of wood have been
woven together with iron straps, forming an extremely sturdy floor. In the back
corner of the tent, a wide piece of fabric has been attached to the tent-top,
forming a wall of sorts. The wall has been partially pushed to one side, and
behind it sits a small cot.
name: Commander's Tent

desc:
This room reeks of mould, decay and human sweat. The source of the pungent odour
is not apparent although, as the room appears to be fairly clean. Racks hanging
from the four wooden walls are resting places for weapons and shields. Small
cots, lined up along the front and rear walls, are neatly covered with grey
blankets that look clean enough. At the foot of each bed, a wooden chest sits
closed with a small lock on most. Other than the inadequate lighting for human
eyes, provided by tiny lanterns dangling from the ceiling, the entire barrack
appears in order. The source of the mysterious odour is nowhere to be seen.
name: Dimly Lit Barracks

desc:
A small courtyard has been formed by earthen walls surrounding this area. The
piled up dirt is supported at the corners by wooden beams but otherwise all that
protects this encampment is well-packed soil. Inside the battlements to the
north, a small hut has been crafted out of an assortment of spare wood, as has a
shack directly east. These structures have a haphazard appearance, being made up
of whatever was lying around at the time. The only sturdy wood in this camp
rests against the earthen walls, in the shape of well-constructed ladders.
name: Mustering Grounds

desc:
The architects of this fort must have had a limited supply of wood with which to
construct it. A storage room would have been the last priority in a project such
as this. Nevertheless, here stands a storage area made entirely out of earth.
Small walls have been crafted from heavily packed dirt to form the supports for
the roof. The roof itself is nothing more than heavily oiled hides, stretched
across the walls and held in place by some sort of weight atop the edges.
Nothing sits upon the floor for fear of water damage. All the weapons, armour
and foodstuffs sit upon makeshift shelving crafted from piles of dirt and long
wooden planks.
name: Storage Room

desc:
This hastily built mess hall seems the worse for wear. Irregular shaped gaps in
every wall allow the light from outside to shine in, and shoddy patching of
holes in the roof threaten to drench diners with every storm. But in stark
contrast, the stove in the northwest corner of the hut is in pristine condition,
clean and well built. It is not new, but cared for very well. This speaks
volumes about the priorities of this fort's inhabitants. In the middle of the
room, long but very narrow tables have been set with small stools placed
underneath.
name: Mess Hall

desc:
A wide, well-kept road runs between two fortified encampments. On the north and
south sides of the road, the two earth forts face each other like near mirror
images. Two heavily reinforced wooden doors lead into their respective forts.
Massive earthen walls that would tower above even the tallest troll run most of
the length of the eyot and are topped with wooden spikes. On the eastern and
western sides of the island, smooth, white pebbles form wide fords, leading to
either riverbank. The sound of rushing waters from the Isen, slamming into the
northern banks of the eyot, can be heard day and night.
name: Eyot in the Isen

desc:
The Great South Road runs directly into the waters of Sir Angren, forming one of
two large fords. White, smooth cobblestones have been stacked onto the riverbed,
allowing easy passage for large numbers of people or animals attempting to cross
the swift waters. The ford doesn't completely cross the river, but leads to a
small eyot, centred in the Isen. The river runs around the eyot on either side,
forming large streams. Upon the eyot, two massive earthen forts stand guard over
a wide road that leads between them.
name: Fording the Isen

desc:
This small and filthy dungeon is damp and quickly dug. It is used to keep
criminals for shorter periods of time. A pile of refuse and rubbish has
collected in a corner, barely covered by dirt. In another corner, water from the
surrounding earth has seeped in, forming a small puddle of cold water.
name: A Filthy Dungeon

desc:
This small and dusty barn is the perfect place to house some of the animals for
the farm. Also kept here is animal feed and farming tools. There is a line of
stalls along the west wall where the horses are kept. Hanging near these stalls
are the bridles, and on a stand below that some saddles.
name: A Small Barn

desc:
The tunnel reveals many signs of mining. Although a great amount of work must
have been needed to seek the wealth in the mountain, there is no obvious sign of
ore here. The walls are smooth and hard, and impenetrable except for the eastern
wall, which seems to have been blocked by some iron bars. The cavern comes to an
end here. The ground underfoot is smooth and is covered by dirt.
name: End of the Cave
